subtopic_detail: NCSP follow-up of higher-risk HPV positive primary screening
stem: You are the general practitioner reviewing a 38-year-old woman whose 5-yearly cervical screening test has just returned. She has had three previous normal Pap smears, is not pregnant, has no abnormal bleeding and is in a stable monogamous relationship. The cervical screening test detected HPV other than 16 and 18, and the liquid-based reflex cytology was negative for intraepithelial lesion or malignancy. She is asking about what should happen next under the National Cervical Screening Program.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6 °C, HR 70, RR 14, SpO2 99% room air, BP 118/72 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Unremarkable bimanual examination, cervix appears smooth on speculum, no contact bleeding, no discharge
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening test HPV (not 16/18) detected, reflex liquid-based cytology negative for intraepithelial lesion or malignancy, HIV negative, no immunosuppression

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step under the National Cervical Screening Program?

options.text: Refer immediately to a gynaecologist for diagnostic colposcopy with directed biopsy regardless of cytology to confirm presence or absence of high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia and exclude occult invasive disease
options.rationale: Colposcopy is indicated for HPV 16/18 detection or for any HPV with possible high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion or worse on reflex cytology, not for HPV other with negative cytology. (Source: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)
source_refs: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test in 12 months to assess HPV persistence; refer for colposcopy if HPV is still detected at the 12-month repeat test or if any cytology becomes pHSIL or HSIL on reflex testing
options.rationale: Under the NCSP intermediate-risk pathway, HPV other detected with negative reflex cytology is followed by a repeat HPV test in 12 months; persistence at that point triggers colposcopy referral. (Source: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)
source_refs: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines
Cancer Council Australia: Cervical Cancer Screening

options.text: Reassure her, return her to routine 5-yearly cervical screening because the reflex cytology was negative for intraepithelial lesion or malignancy and the HPV strain detected was a lower-oncogenic-risk non-16/18 type
options.rationale: HPV detected (any type) returns the patient to a 12-month follow-up, not 5-yearly screening. (Source: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)
source_refs: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines

options.text: Arrange a self-collected vaginal swab for HPV testing within 3 months as a confirmatory specimen because of a meaningful false-positive rate on practitioner-collected primary cervical samples in the screening setting
options.rationale: Self-collection is offered as an alternative primary screening option, not a confirmatory test after a positive practitioner-collected specimen. (Source: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)
source_refs: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines

options.text: Prescribe topical imiquimod 5% cream three nights per week for 16 weeks to assist HPV clearance and arrange a repeat cervical screening test after treatment is complete with referral if persistent positivity remains
options.rationale: Topical imiquimod is not part of standard cervical screening management; clearance of HPV is monitored by repeat testing alone. (Source: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)
source_refs: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines

explanation.summary: Under the Australian National Cervical Screening Program, primary HPV testing is performed 5-yearly from age 25. HPV 16 or 18 detected is high risk and triggers immediate colposcopy referral. HPV other (non-16/18) detected with negative or low-grade reflex cytology is intermediate risk and triggers a repeat HPV test in 12 months. Persistent HPV at 12 months prompts colposcopy referral. Self-collection is an alternative primary screening option, not a confirmatory test.
key_points: Primary HPV testing 5-yearly from age 25
HPV 16/18 detected: refer for colposcopy
HPV other with negative or low-grade cytology: repeat HPV in 12 months
Persistent HPV at 12 months: refer for colposcopy
Self-collected swab is alternative primary screening, not confirmatory
explanation.pearls: Co-test with cytology is reserved for symptomatic women, follow-up of treated high-grade lesions, immunosuppressed women, and DES-exposed women; routine screening is HPV alone with reflex cytology only when HPV is detected.

sources.label: NCSP National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines
Cancer Council Australia: Cervical Cancer Screening

subtopic_detail: Anorexia nervosa medical admission criteria and refeeding syndrome
stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 19-year-old non-Aboriginal woman with a 3-year history of anorexia nervosa restricting subtype. Her parents have brought her in because of progressive fatigue, dizziness on standing and one syncopal episode while showering this morning. She has been eating around 400 kcal per day for the past month. She is medically stable but markedly underweight. She has been seeing an outpatient psychologist weekly but has not been admitted before.

data_table.Vital signs: T 35.6 °C, HR 38 supine resting, RR 14, SpO2 98% room air, BP 86/52 lying 70/40 standing, weight 38 kg height 168 cm BMI 13.5 kg/m2
data_table.Examination: Cachectic, lanugo on arms and back, cool peripheries, no oedema, alert and orientated but withdrawn, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Na 134 K 2.9 Cl 96 HCO3 28 mmol/L, phosphate 0.65 mmol/L, magnesium 0.62 mmol/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L, ALT 78 ALP 88 albumin 32 g/L, ECG QTc 478 ms, FBC unremarkable

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management plan?

options.text: Discharge home with daily dietitian and psychologist input, immediate commencement of a high calorie oral nutritional supplement plan of 3000 kcal/day and review by adolescent psychiatry in the eating disorders day program within 48 hours
options.rationale: BMI 13.5 with bradycardia, hypotension, hypokalaemia, hypophosphataemia and QTc prolongation all meet inpatient admission criteria for anorexia nervosa. (Source: ANZAED Treatment Guidelines)

options.text: Admit to a general medical ward for cardiac monitoring, commence high-calorie nasogastric feeding at 30 kcal/kg/day, correct potassium and phosphate aggressively and start a benzodiazepine at night to manage refeeding anxiety
options.rationale: Starting feeds at 30 kcal/kg/day in a high-risk patient risks refeeding syndrome; initial feeds are 5-10 kcal/kg/day with cautious escalation. (Source: ANZAED Treatment Guidelines)

options.text: Admit to a monitored medical bed, commence oral thiamine 200 mg daily and a multivitamin, replace potassium phosphate and magnesium before feeding, start cautious refeeding at 5 to 10 kcal/kg/day and increase under daily electrolyte monitoring
options.rationale: Inpatient management of severe anorexia with multiple medical criteria includes thiamine, electrolyte correction before feeding and cautious refeeding starting at 5-10 kcal/kg/day with daily phosphate, potassium, magnesium and glucose monitoring. (Source: ANZAED Treatment Guidelines)
source_refs: MARSIPAN UK adapted Australian medical care of severe anorexia

options.text: Admit to the high-dependency unit, commence rapid parenteral nutrition at 40 kcal/kg/day to restore weight gain, correct electrolytes intravenously and request urgent compulsory inpatient treatment under the relevant state Mental Health Act
options.rationale: Parenteral nutrition is not first-line; oral or nasogastric refeeding is preferred. Use of the Mental Health Act is reserved for refusal of life-saving treatment without insight. (Source: ANZAED Treatment Guidelines)

options.text: Refer urgently to a private eating disorders inpatient program with daily outpatient electrolyte monitoring at her general practice surgery while a private psychiatric admission is being arranged over the next 7 days
options.rationale: Acute medical instability requires immediate medical admission rather than outpatient bridging while awaiting a private bed. (Source: ANZAED Treatment Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Medical admission criteria for anorexia nervosa include BMI under 14, bradycardia under 40, postural drop, hypothermia, electrolyte derangement, QTc over 450 and recent syncope. Inpatient care prioritises cautious refeeding to avoid refeeding syndrome, starting at 5-10 kcal/kg/day with daily electrolyte monitoring. Phosphate, potassium and magnesium are replaced before and during feeding, with prophylactic thiamine. Use of the Mental Health Act is reserved for refusal of life-saving treatment.
key_points: Admit if BMI under 14, HR under 40, postural drop, QTc over 450 or electrolyte derangement
Refeed at 5-10 kcal/kg/day, escalate slowly with daily electrolytes
Replace phosphate, potassium, magnesium before and during feeds
Thiamine 200 mg daily prophylaxis
Mental Health Act for refusal of life-saving treatment only
explanation.pearls: Phosphate is the single most reliable early biochemical marker of refeeding syndrome; a drop below 0.6 mmol/L in the first week of refeeding warrants dose reduction, supplementation and senior review.

sources.label: MARSIPAN UK adapted Australian medical care of severe anorexia

subtopic_detail: Complex PTSD therapy and pharmacotherapy choice
stem: You are the general practitioner reviewing a 34-year-old non-Aboriginal woman 4 months after a violent assault. She has nightmares, hypervigilance, intrusive flashbacks during the day, avoidance of public transport and difficulty sleeping. She has tried sertraline at 50 mg daily for the last 6 weeks with partial response on mood symptoms but persistent flashbacks. She is engaged with a clinical psychologist for supportive counselling and is willing to consider trauma-focused therapy. She has no current suicidal ideation and is working part-time.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 °C, HR 84, RR 16, SpO2 99% room air, BP 122/76 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Anxious affect with intermittent tearfulness, no psychomotor retardation, intact insight and judgement, no current suicidality
data_table.Investigations: PCL-5 score 48 (moderate-severe), PHQ-9 score 18 (moderate-severe), thyroid function and FBC within reference ranges, no substance use on history or urine drug screen

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Switch sertraline to mirtazapine 30 mg nocte targeting sleep and appetite improvement, and continue supportive counselling with the clinical psychologist as the sole talking therapy intervention going forward
options.rationale: Mirtazapine is not first-line in PTSD and supportive counselling alone is inferior to trauma-focused therapy for core PTSD symptoms. (Source: Phoenix Australia Australian PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Add olanzapine 5 mg nocte for adjunctive sleep and re-experiencing symptom control, and review SSRI dose titration in 4 weeks before considering any psychological therapy referral or non-pharmacological intervention
options.rationale: Atypical antipsychotic augmentation is third-line in PTSD and carries metabolic risk; first-line escalation is trauma-focused psychological therapy. (Source: Phoenix Australia Australian PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Increase sertraline to 100 mg daily, add prazosin 1 mg nocte for nightmares titrated to 5 mg over 4 weeks and review in 4 weeks for further dose adjustment without any change to her current psychological treatment
options.rationale: Prazosin is an option for refractory nightmares but does not substitute for first-line trauma-focused psychological therapy in PTSD. (Source: Phoenix Australia Australian PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for trauma-focused CBT or EMDR with an experienced clinician, continue sertraline at 50 mg with a planned review of dose at the next appointment and arrange GP review in 4 weeks to monitor symptoms
options.rationale: Australian PTSD guidelines place trauma-focused CBT and EMDR as first-line therapy; supportive counselling is insufficient and pharmacotherapy is second-line or adjunctive. (Source: Phoenix Australia Australian PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Recommend a 6-week course of mindfulness-based stress reduction in a community group programme as the primary therapy and discontinue sertraline because of the partial response and intolerance of medication side effects
options.rationale: MBSR is not first-line for PTSD and SSRI discontinuation in partial responders is not warranted; trauma-focused therapy is the next step. (Source: Phoenix Australia Australian PTSD Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Australian guidelines for PTSD recommend trauma-focused cognitive behavioural therapy or eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing as first-line treatment. SSRIs (sertraline or paroxetine) are second-line or used as adjuncts when trauma-focused therapy is unavailable or ineffective. Prazosin can target nightmares in refractory cases. Atypical antipsychotic augmentation is reserved for partial response after first- and second-line measures. Mindfulness and supportive counselling alone are not sufficient.
key_points: Trauma-focused CBT and EMDR are first-line in PTSD
Sertraline and paroxetine are second-line SSRIs
Prazosin can target nightmares in refractory cases
Atypical antipsychotic augmentation is third-line
Supportive counselling alone is inadequate for core PTSD symptoms
explanation.pearls: Eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing (EMDR) is non-inferior to trauma-focused CBT and may be preferred when patients find prolonged narrative exposure too distressing; benzodiazepines are not recommended in PTSD because they worsen recovery.

stem: You are the medical registrar admitting a 56-year-old non-Aboriginal man with a 30-year history of heavy alcohol use. He has been on the ward for 36 hours after self-presenting for detoxification and stopped drinking 48 hours ago. He is now confused, tremulous, sweating profusely and has had a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure 30 minutes ago. He is intermittently agitated and seeing animals on the curtains. He has had two previous episodes of complicated alcohol withdrawal.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.2 °C, HR 132, RR 26, SpO2 96% room air, BP 168/108 mmHg, capillary glucose 4.8 mmol/L
data_table.Examination: Disoriented to time and place, marked tremor, profuse diaphoresis, visual hallucinations, GCS 14, no focal neurology, soft non-tender abdomen
data_table.Investigations: Na 132 K 3.2 Mg 0.62 phosphate 0.7 mmol/L, glucose 4.8 mmol/L, urea 7.2 creatinine 92 micromol/L, ALT 96 GGT 540 U/L, INR 1.4, FBC platelets 102 x10^9/L, head CT no acute pathology

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Commence oral diazepam 20 mg every 2 hours by symptom-triggered CIWA-Ar protocol on the general ward and arrange routine medical review by the consultant on the morning ward round in approximately 12 hours from now
options.rationale: Established delirium tremens with seizure and autonomic instability requires intravenous benzodiazepine titration in a high-dependency setting, not oral symptom-triggered dosing on a general ward. (Source: RACGP Alcohol Use Disorders)

options.text: Administer haloperidol 5 mg intramuscular for agitation, continue oral thiamine 100 mg daily and observe for further seizures on the ward with intramuscular diazepam available for clinician-initiated breakthrough as needed
options.rationale: First-generation antipsychotics lower seizure threshold and are inappropriate as primary therapy in delirium tremens; benzodiazepines remain the cornerstone. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Start phenytoin loading dose 18 mg/kg over 30 minutes for ongoing seizure prevention and continue an oral benzodiazepine taper with no need to escalate the level of care beyond the current general medical ward bed
options.rationale: Phenytoin is not effective for alcohol withdrawal seizures and does not treat the underlying autonomic instability; benzodiazepine titration is first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Transfer to the intensive care unit for sedation with continuous propofol infusion and elective intubation to control airway and autonomic features given the established delirium tremens with seizure and pyrexia
options.rationale: Intubation and propofol are reserved for refractory delirium tremens after high-dose benzodiazepine titration has failed; titration is the first step. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Transfer to a high-dependency or intensive care bed, give intravenous diazepam 10 mg titrated to RASS minus 1, administer parenteral thiamine 300 mg three times daily for 3-5 days before any glucose-containing fluids and replace magnesium and potassium
options.rationale: Established delirium tremens with seizure mandates HDU care, IV benzodiazepine titration, parenteral thiamine before glucose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy and electrolyte correction. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Delirium tremens is a medical emergency with mortality up to 15% if untreated. Management requires high-dependency or intensive care monitoring, intravenous benzodiazepine titration (diazepam or midazolam), parenteral thiamine 300 mg three times daily before any glucose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy, and electrolyte correction. Antipsychotics are avoided as primary therapy because they lower the seizure threshold. Intubation and propofol are reserved for refractory cases.
key_points: DT requires HDU or ICU monitoring
IV benzodiazepine titration is first-line
Parenteral thiamine 300 mg TDS before glucose
Correct magnesium, potassium, phosphate
Avoid first-generation antipsychotics as primary therapy
explanation.pearls: Score severity of withdrawal with CIWA-Ar but use the score to titrate benzodiazepine dose rather than to triage location; any seizure, autonomic instability or hallucinations escalate the patient to high-dependency or intensive care regardless of the numeric score.

stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 28-year-old non-Aboriginal man who presents requesting an assessment for adult ADHD. He describes lifelong difficulties with sustained attention, frequent forgetfulness, restlessness, impulsivity at work, and three traffic infringements in the past 12 months for inattention while driving. He has not been previously assessed. Collateral from his mother confirms problems since primary school. He drinks alcohol on weekends only and uses no other substances. He has no current depression or anxiety on screening.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 °C, HR 76, RR 14, SpO2 99% room air, BP 124/78 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Engaging in conversation, mildly restless, no thought disorder, no psychotic features, GCS 15, normal cardiac and neurological examination
data_table.Investigations: DIVA-5 structured interview supports ADHD combined presentation, ASRS-v1.1 screener positive in both inattentive and hyperactive-impulsive domains, no medical contraindications on baseline ECG and bloods, urine drug screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step before initiating stimulant medication?

options.text: Refer to a psychiatrist or paediatrician with an authority to prescribe stimulant medication for confirmation of diagnosis and treatment initiation, and counsel on Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive obligations including notifying the driver licensing authority
options.rationale: Stimulant initiation requires a stimulant authority (psychiatrist or paediatrician) in Australia and Austroads requires patient notification of the driver licensing authority for treated ADHD with safety-relevant features. (Source: Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive 2022)

options.text: Commence methylphenidate immediate release 10 mg twice daily under your own prescription, with cardiac and blood pressure monitoring at 2 and 4 weeks, and continue community follow-up before any psychiatry referral is considered
options.rationale: General practitioners cannot independently initiate stimulant therapy for adult ADHD in Australia; this requires a stimulant authority. (Source: PBS Stimulants for ADHD authority)

options.text: Trial atomoxetine 40 mg daily for 6 weeks first and refer for stimulant prescriber only if atomoxetine has failed to control core ADHD symptoms because stimulants carry abuse potential and cardiovascular risk concerns
options.rationale: Stimulants are first-line for adult ADHD in Australia; atomoxetine is second-line when stimulants are contraindicated or not tolerated. (Source: RACGP: Adult ADHD)

options.text: Recommend cognitive behavioural therapy alone for 12 weeks with no pharmacotherapy until psychological therapy completion and review CBT outcomes before considering whether stimulant medication will be necessary at that future review
options.rationale: CBT is adjunctive in moderate-severe adult ADHD; pharmacotherapy is the primary intervention with greatest effect size. (Source: RACGP: Adult ADHD)

options.text: Advise the patient that ADHD diagnosis in adulthood requires symptoms appearing before age 7 years and cannot be diagnosed with first presentation at 28 years even with childhood history confirmed by collateral parental information
options.rationale: DSM-5 lowered the age-of-onset criterion to before age 12 and the diagnosis can be confirmed in adulthood with appropriate collateral. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD diagnosis requires a structured assessment, evidence of symptoms before age 12, functional impairment across two or more settings, and exclusion of mimics. Stimulant medication is first-line treatment and requires a stimulant authority held by a psychiatrist or paediatrician. Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive obligates notification of the driver licensing authority when ADHD has safety-relevant features or pharmacotherapy is being commenced. CBT and skills coaching are adjunctive.
key_points: DSM-5 onset before age 12 with cross-setting impairment
Stimulants are first-line; authority required to prescribe
Atomoxetine is second-line when stimulants contraindicated
Austroads driver licensing notification for safety-relevant ADHD
CBT and coaching are adjunctive
explanation.pearls: Baseline ECG is recommended before stimulant initiation in patients with family history of sudden cardiac death, structural heart disease, syncope, palpitations or pre-existing hypertension; stimulant cardiovascular safety in healthy adults is otherwise good.
